blocks.text: Logistic Regression is a classification algorithm (despite the name) that models the probability that an input belongs to a class. It applies the sigmoid function to a linear combination of features: P(y=1|x) = σ(wᵀx + b) = 1 / (1 + e^(−wᵀx−b)). Output is always between 0 and 1 (probability). Threshold (usually 0.5) converts probability to class label. Training minimises Binary Cross-Entropy loss (log-loss): L = −(y×log(ŷ) + (1−y)×log(1−ŷ)). For multi-class: Softmax function + Categorical Cross-Entropy. Assumptions: linearity between log-odds and features, independence of observations, no severe multicollinearity. Despite simplicity, logistic regression is a strong baseline — in many real problems it matches or beats complex models. Output probabilities are well-calibrated (unlike SVMs). Training: O(n×d) per gradient descent step.

Logistic regression is a linear classifier that draws a straight dividing line (or hyperplane in higher dimensions) between classes. Points on one side are class 0, the other side class 1. The sigmoid function converts the signed distance from the line into a probability: very far on the positive side → near 1, very far on the negative side → near 0, on the line → exactly 0.5.

Logistic regression outputs PROBABILITIES, not classes. This makes it useful for risk scores, calibration, and thresholding. The decision boundary is always linear (a hyperplane). If the classes are not linearly separable, add polynomial features or switch to a non-linear model. L1 logistic regression = automatic feature selection.

qas.q: Why is it called 'logistic regression' if it is used for classification?
qas.a: It models the log-odds (logit) = log(p/(1-p)) as a linear function of features — hence 'regression' in the sense of modelling a continuous quantity (the log-odds). The logistic (sigmoid) function maps the output back to a probability. The classification decision uses a threshold on that probability.

qas.q: Why is cross-entropy used as the loss for logistic regression and not MSE?
qas.a: MSE applied to sigmoid outputs creates a non-convex loss with many local minima. Cross-entropy loss, combined with the sigmoid activation, produces a convex loss function with a single global minimum — gradient descent converges reliably. Cross-entropy also has a nice gradient: ∂L/∂w = (ŷ−y)×x, which is identical in form to linear regression's gradient.

qas.q: How does logistic regression handle multi-class classification?
qas.a: Two approaches: One-vs-Rest (OvR) — train K binary classifiers, one per class against all others. Predict the class whose classifier gives the highest score. Softmax regression (multinomial logistic regression) — a single model with K output nodes using softmax: P(yₖ|x) = e^(wₖᵀx) / Σe^(wⱼᵀx). Softmax is preferred as it outputs a proper probability distribution over all classes.

blocks.text: Logistic regression is the first ML algorithm most interviewers ask about. The strong answer covers: sigmoid function, cross-entropy loss (and why not MSE), linear decision boundary, when it fails (non-linearly separable data), and that regularised logistic regression (L1) performs built-in feature selection.

K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN) is a non-parametric, instance-based algorithm. It makes predictions by finding the K most similar training examples to a query point (using a distance metric like Euclidean or Manhattan distance) and returning the majority class (classification) or mean value (regression) of those K neighbours. KNN has no training phase — it memorises the entire training set. All computation happens at prediction time. Prediction complexity: O(n×d) per query (must scan all training points). For large datasets, use KD-trees or Ball trees: O(log n) per query for low dimensions. Key hyperparameters: K (number of neighbours) and the distance metric. K=1 overfits (pure memorisation). K=n underfits (predicts global majority). Choose K via cross-validation (odd K for binary to avoid ties). Critical: features must be scaled before using KNN — without scaling, high-magnitude features dominate the distance calculation.

KNN is like asking your K nearest neighbours for advice on a decision. If 4 out of 5 neighbours say 'buy an umbrella', you buy one. The algorithm is purely social — it has no internal model of the world, just looks at what similar people (nearby training points) did. Smart in communities (dense data), confused in the wilderness (sparse data).

KNN fails in high dimensions (curse of dimensionality), requires scaling, is slow at prediction for large datasets, and cannot handle missing values. KNN advantage: naturally handles multi-class, non-linear boundaries, requires no training, adapts as new data is added (just add to the set). Use KNN as a baseline for smaller datasets (<100k samples, <50 features).

qas.q: What is the effect of changing K in KNN?
qas.a: Small K (K=1): very flexible decision boundary, low bias, high variance — overfits, sensitive to noise. Large K: smooth decision boundary, high bias, low variance — underfits, averages over too many points. K=1 memorises, K=n predicts global majority. Optimal K found via cross-validation — typically tested over a range [1, 3, 5, 7, 11, 15, 21].

qas.q: Why is feature scaling mandatory for KNN?
qas.a: KNN uses Euclidean distance: √(Σ(xᵢ-yᵢ)²). A feature with range 0-100,000 (income) will completely dominate a feature with range 0-1 (binary flag). Scaling ensures all features contribute proportionally to the distance calculation. Without scaling, KNN is effectively using only the unscaled features.

qas.q: What is the time complexity of KNN at inference time?
qas.a: Naive implementation: O(n×d) per query — must compute distance to all n training points across d features. With a KD-tree: O(d×log n) for low dimensions (d < 20). With a Ball tree: O(d×log n) for higher dimensions. For very large datasets (millions of points), approximate nearest neighbour libraries (FAISS, HNSW) reduce this to sub-linear time.

blocks.text: KNN is used to test understanding of instance-based learning, lazy vs eager models, and the curse of dimensionality. The practical question: 'Would you use KNN for a dataset with 1M rows and 200 features?' No — too slow at inference, too high-dimensional. Use tree-based models or neural networks instead.

Naive Bayes is a probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' theorem with a strong (naive) independence assumption. Bayes' theorem: P(class|features) = P(features|class) × P(class) / P(features). The 'naive' assumption: all features are conditionally independent given the class. This makes the joint probability tractable: P(features|class) = Π P(featureᵢ|class). Despite this rarely-true assumption, Naive Bayes works surprisingly well in practice. Variants: Gaussian NB (continuous features, assumes Gaussian distribution), Multinomial NB (count features, ideal for text classification — word counts), Bernoulli NB (binary features). Advantages: extremely fast (O(n×d) training, O(d×K) prediction), works well with small training data, handles high-dimensional data well (text), naturally handles multi-class. Weakness: independence assumption often violated in practice — correlated features degrade performance.

Naive Bayes is like a spam filter that asks: 'What is the probability this email is spam, given it contains the words FREE and CLICK?' It assumes the presence of FREE and CLICK are independent of each other (which is naive — spam emails tend to have both together). Despite this, it identifies spam accurately because even a wrong independence assumption preserves the dominant signal.

Naive Bayes is the go-to algorithm for text classification. It works well when: training data is small, features are independent (or nearly), real-time prediction needed, multi-class problem. It fails when: features are highly correlated, continuous features have complex distributions, you need calibrated probabilities (the naive assumption distorts them).

qas.q: Why is Naive Bayes good for text classification?
qas.a: Text as bag-of-words produces high-dimensional, sparse feature vectors (one dimension per word in vocabulary). Multinomial NB handles sparsity naturally, is fast (O(n×d)), and the independence assumption roughly holds (word frequencies are weakly correlated). It also needs very little data per class — the per-word probabilities P(word|class) are estimated reliably even with thousands of classes.

qas.q: What is Laplace smoothing and why is it needed?
qas.a: If a word appears in test data but never appeared with a given class in training data, P(word|class)=0. Multiplying P(word|class)×P(other features|class)=0 — the probability of any class becomes 0, dominated by this one unseen word. Laplace smoothing adds a small constant α (usually 1) to all counts: P(wordᵢ|class) = (count(wordᵢ,class) + α) / (total_words_in_class + α×vocab_size). This prevents zero-probability catastrophes.

blocks.text: Naive Bayes questions often appear alongside text classification system design: 'Build a spam filter.' Strong answer: use Multinomial NB as baseline (fast, no scaling needed, handles high dimensions), explain Laplace smoothing, mention that Logistic Regression or BERT would be stronger at scale.

A Decision Tree is a hierarchical model that makes decisions by recursively splitting the data based on feature thresholds. Each internal node is a question (feature < threshold?), each branch is an answer (yes/no), each leaf is a prediction (class label for classification, mean for regression). Tree learning is greedy: at each step, choose the split that maximises information gain (classification) or minimises MSE (regression). Information Gain = Parent impurity − weighted average of child impurities. Impurity measures: Gini Impurity = 1 - Σ(pᵢ²) — minimising Gini, or Entropy = -Σ(pᵢ × log₂pᵢ) — maximising information gain. Advantages: interpretable (can visualise the tree), handles both numeric and categorical features, no scaling needed, handles non-linearity, fast prediction O(log n). Weaknesses: prone to overfitting (grows infinitely without constraints), high variance (small data changes → very different tree). Control with: max_depth, min_samples_split, min_samples_leaf.

A decision tree is like the 20 Questions game. 'Is the animal bigger than a car?' [yes] → 'Does it have a trunk?' [yes] → Elephant! Each question partitions the possibilities. The tree learns which questions are most discriminating and asks them in order. A well-pruned tree is a readable decision flowchart a human can follow.

Gini vs Entropy: both work similarly in practice. Gini is faster to compute (no logarithm). Entropy is slightly more theoretically grounded (information theory). sklearn's DecisionTreeClassifier uses Gini by default. Max_depth is the most important hyperparameter — even max_depth=3-5 often generalises better than a full tree.

qas.q: How does a decision tree decide which feature to split on?
qas.a: It tries every possible split on every feature and picks the one that maximises the information gain (or minimises Gini impurity). For a continuous feature, it sorts the values and tries each midpoint as a threshold — O(n log n) per feature. For a categorical feature with K values, it tries subsets. The split maximising the purity improvement is chosen.

qas.q: Why do decision trees overfit and how do you prevent it?
qas.a: Without constraints, a tree will keep splitting until every leaf contains exactly one training point — perfect training accuracy, terrible generalisation. Prevention: max_depth (limits depth), min_samples_split (minimum samples to split a node), min_samples_leaf (minimum samples in a leaf), max_features (limits features considered per split). Post-pruning: grow full tree then prune branches that don't improve validation performance.

qas.q: What is the difference between Gini impurity and entropy as split criteria?
qas.a: Both measure class impurity in a node. Gini = 1 - Σpᵢ² (range 0 to 0.5 for binary). Entropy = -Σpᵢlog₂pᵢ (range 0 to 1). Gini tends to isolate the most frequent class in its own branch. Entropy is more balanced. In practice, both give very similar trees. Gini is preferred computationally (no log computation).

blocks.text: Decision trees are foundational for understanding ensemble methods. Know: information gain / Gini, overfitting and pruning, that no scaling is needed, that depth is the key hyperparameter. Trees are rarely used alone in production — they are almost always used as base learners in Random Forest or Gradient Boosting.

Support Vector Machine (SVM) finds the hyperplane that separates classes with the maximum margin. The margin is the distance between the hyperplane and the nearest training points (support vectors). Maximising the margin improves generalisation. Hard-margin SVM: assumes classes are linearly separable. Soft-margin SVM: allows some misclassifications via slack variables, controlled by hyperparameter C. C large → small margin, fewer training errors (may overfit). C small → large margin, more training errors (more regularised). Kernel trick: maps data to higher dimensions implicitly (without computing the full transformation) using a kernel function K(x,z) = φ(x)ᵀφ(z). Common kernels: Linear, Polynomial, RBF (Radial Basis Function / Gaussian). RBF kernel: K(x,z) = exp(−γ||x−z||²). Hyperparameters for RBF-SVM: C (margin vs misclassification tradeoff) and γ (width of the Gaussian — small γ = broad, large γ = narrow). Training complexity: O(n²) to O(n³) — does not scale to large datasets (>100k samples).

SVM is like finding the widest street between two neighbourhoods. The street is the decision boundary; the buildings on either side that touch the curb are the support vectors. Wider street = more confident separation = better generalisation. The kernel trick is like lifting both neighbourhoods into 3D where they become separable even if they are tangled in 2D.

SVM key points: maximum-margin classifier, support vectors are the only training points that matter (rest can be removed without changing the model), kernel trick enables non-linear boundaries without explicit transformation, does not scale to large n (O(n³) training). For large datasets, use logistic regression or tree ensembles. SVM still excellent for small/medium datasets, text classification (high-dimensional linear SVM), and problems with clear margin separation.

qas.q: What are support vectors?
qas.a: Support vectors are the training data points that lie closest to the decision boundary (within the margin or on the margin boundary). They are the only points that determine the position and orientation of the hyperplane. All other training points could be removed and the model would be identical. This sparsity is a key theoretical property.

qas.q: What does the C parameter control in SVM?
qas.a: C is the penalty for misclassification (regularisation parameter). High C: minimises training errors, narrows margin → overfitting risk. Low C: allows more misclassifications, widens margin → more regularised. C=∞ is hard-margin SVM (no misclassifications allowed). Tune C via cross-validation on a log-scale grid: [0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100].

qas.q: When would you use an RBF kernel vs a linear kernel?
qas.a: Linear kernel: when data is linearly separable in the original feature space, or when dimensionality is very high (text — thousands of features). Linear SVM is O(n×d) and scales well. RBF kernel: when data has non-linear boundaries, lower-dimensional feature space. RBF SVM requires choosing γ in addition to C, and is O(n²-n³) — unsuitable for large n.

blocks.text: SVM questions test maximum-margin intuition, kernel trick, and scaling limitations. The practical question: 'Would you use SVM for 10M training samples?' No — O(n³) training is infeasible. Use logistic regression or gradient boosting. SVM remains relevant for: small/medium datasets, text classification (linear kernel scales with sparse features), or when margin maximisation is theoretically desired.

Choosing the right metric is as important as choosing the right model. Accuracy = (TP + TN) / Total — misleading for imbalanced classes (a model predicting all-negative gets 99% accuracy on a 1% fraud dataset). Precision = TP / (TP + FP) — of all positive predictions, how many were correct? Recall (Sensitivity) = TP / (TP + FN) — of all actual positives, how many did we find? F1 Score = 2 × (Precision × Recall) / (Precision + Recall) — harmonic mean, good for imbalanced classes. ROC-AUC: plots TPR (Recall) vs FPR (FP Rate) at every threshold; AUC = area under curve; 0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect. PR-AUC (Precision-Recall AUC): better than ROC-AUC for highly imbalanced datasets. Confusion Matrix: 2×2 table of TP, TN, FP, FN — the foundation of all binary classification metrics. Precision-Recall tradeoff: increasing threshold → higher precision, lower recall (fewer but more confident positives).

Confusion Matrix (binary):
                 Predicted Positive  Predicted Negative
Actual Positive       TP                   FN
Actual Negative       FP                   TN

Precision = TP / (TP + FP)   ← of predicted positives, how many were right?
Recall    = TP / (TP + FN)   ← of actual positives, how many did we catch?
F1        = 2×P×R / (P+R)    ← harmonic mean
Accuracy  = (TP+TN) / Total  ← overall, misleading if imbalanced

Precision: 'When I shout positive, am I right?' (quality of positive predictions). Recall: 'Did I find all the positives?' (coverage of actual positives). F1: balance between both. Which to prioritise? Precision when false positives are costly (spam filter — don't block real emails). Recall when false negatives are costly (cancer detection — don't miss cancer). AUC-ROC: overall discriminability across all thresholds.

qas.q: When would you use F1 score instead of accuracy?
qas.a: When classes are imbalanced. Example: fraud detection (1% positive). A model predicting all-negative gets 99% accuracy but 0% recall — it misses every fraud. F1 score heavily penalises this: Recall=0 → F1=0. F1 forces the model to make some positive predictions and be correct on them.

qas.q: What is the tradeoff between precision and recall?
qas.a: They move in opposite directions as you adjust the decision threshold. Higher threshold → model only predicts positive when very confident → fewer positives predicted → precision goes up (fewer FPs) but recall drops (more FNs missed). Lower threshold → more positives predicted → recall goes up (catch more true positives) but precision drops (more false alarms). Choose threshold based on the cost of each error type.

qas.q: When is PR-AUC better than ROC-AUC?
qas.a: For highly imbalanced datasets. ROC-AUC can be misleadingly high because it considers TN (which are abundant when negatives dominate). A model that is good at predicting negatives (easy — they are 99% of data) can have high ROC-AUC while missing most positives. PR-AUC ignores TNs entirely and focuses on how well the model handles the minority positive class.

blocks.text: Metrics are asked in almost every ML interview. The trap: saying 'I use accuracy' without asking about class balance. The right answer always starts with: 'What is the class distribution? What is the cost of false positives vs false negatives?' Then choose the metric. This shows production-oriented thinking.

Clustering is an unsupervised learning technique that groups similar data points together without any predefined labels. The algorithm discovers natural groupings in the data based on similarity (distance or density). Unlike classification, there is no 'correct' answer — the quality of clustering depends on how useful the groups are for the downstream task. Applications: customer segmentation (group users by behaviour), anomaly detection (points that don't fit any cluster), document grouping, gene expression analysis, image compression, recommendation systems (recommend items popular in user's cluster). Key distinction from classification: no labels, no ground truth. Evaluation is harder — must use internal metrics (Silhouette score, Elbow method) or downstream task performance. The right number of clusters K is a hyperparameter that must be determined through analysis, not training.

Clustering = find structure in unlabelled data. The 3 main paradigms: Centroid-based (K-Means — assign to nearest centre), Hierarchical (build a tree of merges/splits), Density-based (DBSCAN — find dense regions). Each handles different cluster shapes: K-Means = spherical. DBSCAN = arbitrary shapes. Hierarchical = dendrogram reveals all levels.

qas.q: What is the difference between clustering and classification?
qas.a: Classification is supervised — you have labelled examples and train a model to assign new points to predefined classes. Clustering is unsupervised — no labels exist; the algorithm discovers groups in the data itself. Classification has a clear success metric (accuracy, F1). Clustering success is measured by cohesion/separation metrics or downstream task improvement.

qas.q: How would you use clustering in a real product?
qas.a: Customer segmentation: run K-Means on purchase history + demographics. Each cluster is a customer persona (price-sensitive budget buyer, premium loyalist, one-time buyer). Use clusters to personalise marketing campaigns, pricing, and recommendations. The clusters themselves are the product value — understanding your customer base without manual labelling.

blocks.text: Clustering shows up in ML system design questions: 'How would you personalise content for a new user?' (cluster users, recommend what's popular in their cluster). Always mention that the choice of K and cluster quality metrics are as important as the algorithm itself.

K-Means partitions n data points into K clusters by minimising the within-cluster sum of squared distances to cluster centroids. Algorithm (Lloyd's): (1) Randomly initialise K centroids. (2) Assign each point to the nearest centroid (Euclidean distance). (3) Recompute each centroid as the mean of all assigned points. (4) Repeat steps 2-3 until convergence (assignments stop changing). Convergence is guaranteed but only to a local optimum — sensitive to initialisation. K-Means++ initialisation: choose centroids probabilistically proportional to distance from existing centroids — much better starting points. Time complexity: O(n×K×d×iterations). Assumptions: clusters are spherical (equal variance in all directions), roughly equal size, well-separated. Fails for: elongated clusters, clusters of very different sizes or densities, non-convex cluster shapes.

K-Means is like assigning cities to K regional capitals. Each city belongs to the nearest capital. After assignment, each capital moves to the average location of its cities. Repeat until no city changes its capital. This converges to stable regions — but the final regions depend on where you placed the capitals initially.

K-Means fails for: non-spherical clusters (use DBSCAN), very different cluster sizes (use Gaussian Mixture Models), high-dimensional data (distances become meaningless). K-Means++ solves the bad-initialisation problem. Run K-Means 10+ times with different initialisations and pick the run with lowest inertia (within-cluster sum of squares).

qas.q: How do you choose K in K-Means?
qas.a: Elbow Method: plot inertia (within-cluster sum of squares) vs K. As K increases, inertia decreases. The 'elbow' is where marginal gain from adding more clusters drops sharply. Silhouette Score: measures how similar a point is to its own cluster vs other clusters. Range -1 to 1; higher is better. Choose K that maximises average silhouette. Domain knowledge: if building customer segments for 4 marketing campaigns, K=4.

qas.q: Why is K-Means sensitive to outliers?
qas.a: Centroids are computed as means — means are sensitive to extreme values. One outlier far from any cluster pulls its assigned centroid toward it, distorting the cluster. Solution: K-Medoids (use actual data points as centres, not means — robust to outliers) or remove outliers before clustering.

qas.q: What is K-Means inertia?
qas.a: Inertia = sum of squared distances from each point to its nearest centroid. Lower inertia = more compact clusters. Inertia always decreases as K increases (K=n → inertia=0). This is why you cannot use inertia alone to choose K — you need the Elbow Method to find the diminishing returns point.

blocks.text: K-Means is the most asked clustering algorithm. Know: Lloyd's algorithm, K-Means++ initialisation, elbow method and silhouette score for K selection, limitations (spherical clusters, sensitive to outliers, sensitive to K choice). K-Means is scale-sensitive — always scale features before running.

Hierarchical Clustering builds a tree of clusters (dendrogram) by either merging small clusters up (agglomerative, bottom-up) or splitting large clusters down (divisive, top-down). Agglomerative is far more common. Algorithm: (1) Start: each point is its own cluster. (2) Find the two most similar clusters. (3) Merge them. (4) Repeat until one cluster remains. Linkage criteria determine how cluster distance is measured: Single Linkage — minimum distance between any two points across clusters (creates long chains). Complete Linkage — maximum distance (creates compact, spherical clusters). Average Linkage — mean of all pairwise distances (balance). Ward's Method — minimises total within-cluster variance (most commonly used, tends to produce equally-sized clusters). The dendrogram lets you choose any number of clusters by cutting at different heights — no need to specify K in advance. Time complexity: O(n³) naive; O(n² log n) with optimised algorithms.

Agglomerative clustering is like forming a company through acquisitions. Day 1: every employee is their own team. Each day, the two most similar teams merge. Eventually all teams form one company. The org chart (dendrogram) shows the full history of mergers. Cut the chart at any level to see the team structure at that point in time.

qas.q: What is a dendrogram and how do you use it to determine K?
qas.a: A dendrogram is a tree diagram showing the hierarchy of cluster merges. The y-axis is the distance at which merges happen. To choose K, draw a horizontal line across the dendrogram at a height that cuts K vertical lines. The K clusters below the line are the result. A large vertical gap before a merge indicates natural cluster separation at that level — cut just above the gap.

qas.q: What are the pros and cons of hierarchical clustering vs K-Means?
qas.a: Hierarchical: no need to specify K, produces a full hierarchy (useful for exploration), deterministic (no random init). Cons: O(n³) — does not scale to large datasets. K-Means: O(n×K×d) — scales well, fast. Cons: must specify K, sensitive to initialisation, assumes spherical clusters. Use hierarchical for small datasets where you want to explore cluster structure; K-Means for large datasets.

blocks.text: Hierarchical clustering is asked less often than K-Means but appears in questions about 'what if you don't know K?' The key differentiator: hierarchical needs no K upfront but doesn't scale. Know Ward's linkage (most common in practice) and how to read a dendrogram to pick K.

DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) groups points that are densely packed together and marks points in low-density regions as outliers (noise). Unlike K-Means, DBSCAN discovers arbitrary cluster shapes and automatically identifies outliers. Two hyperparameters: ε (epsilon) — maximum radius of neighbourhood, and minPts — minimum number of points in an ε-neighbourhood to form a core point. Core point: has ≥ minPts points within ε distance. Border point: within ε of a core point but has fewer than minPts neighbours. Noise point: neither core nor border — labelled as outlier (cluster label -1). Algorithm: pick an unvisited core point, expand its cluster by recursively adding all density-reachable points, move to the next unvisited point. Time complexity: O(n log n) with spatial index, O(n²) without. Advantage over K-Means: handles non-spherical clusters, finds outliers automatically, no K needed. Disadvantage: struggles with varying density, sensitive to ε and minPts, does not scale well to high dimensions (distance metrics degrade).

DBSCAN is like finding cities vs wilderness in a country. Dense population centres (core points) form cities. Sparsely populated areas on city outskirts (border points) are suburbs. Remote isolated farmsteads (noise points) are not part of any city. You find cities without knowing how many there are — they emerge from the density of settlement.

DBSCAN key insight: points are either core (dense neighbourhood), border (reachable from a core), or noise (isolated). Cluster label -1 = outlier. This makes DBSCAN a natural anomaly detection algorithm — noise points ARE the anomalies. Use DBSCAN for: geospatial data, anomaly detection, non-convex clusters.

qas.q: How do you choose ε and minPts for DBSCAN?
qas.a: MinPts: rule of thumb = 2×dimensions (for 2D data, minPts=4). Larger minPts = smoother clusters, more noise points. For ε: plot the k-distance graph (distance to kth nearest neighbour for each point, sorted). The 'knee' in the graph suggests a good ε. Grid search with silhouette score can also help but is more expensive.

qas.q: Why is DBSCAN better than K-Means for anomaly detection?
qas.a: K-Means assigns every point to the nearest cluster — it has no concept of outliers. DBSCAN labels low-density points as noise (label -1) automatically, making it a natural outlier detector. In K-Means, an outlier just gets assigned to the nearest cluster and distorts that centroid. In DBSCAN, it gets flagged explicitly.

blocks.text: DBSCAN differentiates ML knowledge. Know: ε and minPts, core/border/noise distinction, that outliers are label -1, and when DBSCAN beats K-Means (non-spherical clusters, anomaly detection, unknown K). The practical limitation: DBSCAN struggles with high-dimensional data because all distances become similar — same curse that breaks KNN.

chapters.title: Evaluating Clusters: Elbow Method and Silhouette Score

blocks.text: Evaluating clustering is harder than supervised learning — there is no ground truth label to compare against. Internal metrics assess cluster quality from the data itself. Elbow Method: plot inertia (within-cluster sum of squares) vs K. As K increases, inertia always drops. Look for the 'elbow' — the K at which the marginal improvement drops sharply. Subjective but widely used. Silhouette Score: for each point i, compute a = mean distance to all points in the same cluster (cohesion) and b = mean distance to all points in the nearest other cluster (separation). Silhouette(i) = (b−a) / max(a,b). Range: +1 (perfectly clustered), 0 (on boundary), -1 (wrongly assigned). Average silhouette across all points gives the overall cluster quality score. Davies-Bouldin Index: ratio of within-cluster scatter to between-cluster separation — lower is better. Calinski-Harabasz Index: ratio of between-cluster variance to within-cluster variance — higher is better.

Silhouette: +1 = perfect (tight cluster, far from others). 0 = indifferent (on the boundary between clusters). -1 = wrong (closer to another cluster's points than its own). Use silhouette to compare K values — pick K with highest average silhouette. Elbow is a quick visual check; silhouette is more principled.

qas.q: How is the silhouette score calculated and what does it mean?
qas.a: For each point: a = mean intra-cluster distance (cohesion — small is good). b = mean distance to nearest other cluster (separation — large is good). Silhouette = (b-a)/max(a,b). Range -1 to +1. Overall score = mean across all points. A score > 0.5 is generally good. Used to compare different K values or clustering algorithms — higher score = better-separated, more compact clusters.

qas.q: Why is inertia alone not enough to choose K?
qas.a: Inertia always decreases as K increases — K=n gives inertia=0 (every point is its own cluster, perfect cohesion but no generalisation). Using inertia alone, you'd always pick the largest K. The elbow method looks for the point of diminishing returns where adding more clusters gives little improvement. Silhouette score additionally penalises poor separation, making it a more complete metric.

blocks.text: Cluster evaluation questions come up when discussing K-Means. Know both methods: Elbow for a quick visual estimate, Silhouette for a quantitative comparison. The interview gotcha: 'Can you use accuracy to evaluate K-Means?' Only if you have ground truth labels (Rand Index, Adjusted Rand Index). Without labels, only internal metrics like Silhouette apply.

Ensemble learning combines predictions from multiple models to produce a better final prediction than any individual model. The intuition: individual models make different errors. Combining them cancels out errors and amplifies correct signals. Three fundamental strategies: Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) — train multiple instances of the same model on different random subsets of the data; average their predictions. Reduces variance. Boosting — train models sequentially where each new model corrects the errors of the previous. Reduces bias. Stacking — train multiple diverse models, then train a meta-model on their outputs to learn how to combine them optimally. Why ensembles work: if base models make uncorrelated errors, averaging them significantly reduces the error. Diversity (different algorithms, different data subsets, different features) is key — an ensemble of identical models is useless.

Ensemble learning is like polling multiple experts instead of relying on one. A single expert may have blind spots, biases, or expertise gaps. Polling 100 diverse experts and taking the majority vote is far more reliable. The more diverse and independently-minded the experts (models), the better the collective decision.

Bagging: parallel training, reduces variance (overfitting). Boosting: sequential training, reduces bias (underfitting). Stacking: uses a meta-model to learn how to blend. In practice: Bagging (Random Forest) is more robust to overfitting. Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM) achieves lower error but risks overfitting. Stacking wins competitions but is complex to implement.

qas.q: Why do ensemble methods outperform individual models?
qas.a: The expected error of an ensemble of M independent models with variance σ² is σ²/M — variance reduces by 1/M. Independence is the key: if models make correlated errors, averaging doesn't help. Ensembles get diversity through: different data subsets (bagging), different features, different algorithms (stacking), sequential error correction (boosting).

qas.q: What is the difference between bagging and boosting?
qas.a: Bagging: trains multiple models independently in parallel on bootstrapped subsets, averages predictions. Reduces variance. Good for high-variance (overfit) models. Boosting: trains models sequentially; each model focuses on the errors of the previous. Reduces bias. Good for high-bias (underfit) models. Bagging is more robust to noise; boosting is more sensitive to outliers since it focuses on hard examples.

blocks.text: Ensemble questions open the door to discussing XGBoost (the most important algorithm in tabular ML competitions). Know: bagging vs boosting distinction, that diversity is the key mechanism, and that XGBoost/LightGBM are gradient boosting variants that dominate Kaggle and production tabular ML.

Bagging trains multiple instances of the same base model on different bootstrap samples (random sampling with replacement) of the training data. Predictions are combined by majority vote (classification) or averaging (regression). Bootstrap sampling: each sample has n rows drawn with replacement from the original n rows. On average, ~63.2% of original rows appear in each bootstrap sample (some rows appear multiple times, some not at all). The ~36.8% of rows not in a bootstrap sample form the Out-of-Bag (OOB) set — can be used as an internal validation set without a separate test set. Why bagging works: each model sees a different subset → makes different errors → averaging cancels errors → reduced variance. Works best with high-variance base learners (unpruned decision trees). Does not significantly change bias. Parallel training makes bagging efficient.

Bagging key points: random data subsets, parallel training, average/vote to combine, reduces variance, OOB samples for free validation. Best base learner for bagging: unpruned deep decision tree (maximum variance, low bias). Averaging many such trees = Random Forest.

qas.q: What is Out-of-Bag (OOB) error estimation?
qas.a: In bagging, each tree is trained on ~63.2% of the training data. The remaining ~36.8% is OOB for that tree. For each training point, average predictions only from trees that did NOT include that point in their bootstrap sample. These are unbiased predictions. OOB error estimates generalisation error without a separate validation set — useful when data is limited.

qas.q: Does bagging reduce bias or variance?
qas.a: Bagging primarily reduces variance. Each model has approximately the same bias as the base learner. Averaging reduces variance by 1/M when models are independent. It does not reduce bias — if the base learner consistently underfits, bagging N underfit models still underfits. This is why bagging works best with low-bias, high-variance models (deep trees).

blocks.text: Bagging is the foundation of Random Forest. Know: bootstrap sampling mechanism, OOB error, variance reduction. The interview follow-up: 'Why does Random Forest add feature randomness on top of bagging?' To further decorrelate trees — without it, all trees might split on the same dominant feature, making them correlated and limiting variance reduction.

Random Forest extends bagging by adding feature randomness: at each split, only a random subset of √d features (classification) or d/3 features (regression) are considered. This decorrelates the trees further — without feature randomness, all trees would tend to split on the strongest features first, making them correlated and limiting variance reduction. Algorithm: (1) Draw bootstrapped dataset. (2) Grow a deep, unpruned decision tree. (3) At each split, consider only √d random features. (4) Repeat M times. (5) Predict by majority vote or average. Key hyperparameters: n_estimators (number of trees — more is better until diminishing returns, usually 100-500), max_features (√d default for classification), max_depth (None = grow fully), min_samples_leaf. Feature importance: Random Forest gives a built-in feature importance score — mean decrease in Gini impurity (or permutation importance, more reliable). Training: O(n × log n × d × M). Random Forest rarely overfits with more trees — adding trees only reduces variance.

Random Forest is like asking 500 specialists each with different expertise and a different patient sample to diagnose a disease. Each doctor (tree) only sees some patients (bootstrap) and specialises in a limited set of symptoms (random features). The majority vote of 500 diverse specialists is far more accurate than any single generalist doctor.

Random Forest strengths: handles missing values (XGBoost does better), no scaling needed, built-in feature importance, parallelisable, rarely overfits (more trees = always as good or better). Weakness: less interpretable than a single tree, slower than a single tree, outperformed by gradient boosting on structured data.

qas.q: Why does Random Forest add feature randomness, not just data randomness?
qas.a: Without feature randomness, all trees would split on the most predictive feature first (e.g., if 'income' is the strongest predictor, every tree starts with it). Correlated trees reduce variance less efficiently — the gains from averaging correlated predictions are diminished. Random feature subsets force different trees to explore different patterns, decorrelating them and enabling the full variance-reduction benefit of averaging.

qas.q: What is permutation feature importance and why is it better than mean decrease in impurity?
qas.a: Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI): averages impurity reduction across all splits where a feature is used. Biased toward high-cardinality features (a feature with many unique values gets more splits, appearing more important artificially). Permutation importance: after training, randomly shuffle feature j, measure performance drop. The drop is the feature's importance. Unbiased, works for any metric, model-agnostic. Slower but more reliable, especially for correlated features.

qas.q: When would you choose XGBoost over Random Forest?
qas.a: XGBoost typically achieves lower error on structured/tabular data because boosting reduces bias (Random Forest only reduces variance). XGBoost also handles missing values natively and provides more hyperparameter control. Random Forest is preferred when: overfitting is a major concern, training speed is critical (RF parallelises fully), or you want OOB error estimates without a validation set.

blocks.text: Random Forest is one of the most important algorithms to know deeply. It serves as the baseline for any tabular ML problem. Know: why feature randomness decorrelates trees, OOB error, feature importance (MDI vs permutation), and the key hyperparameters (n_estimators, max_features, max_depth). Mention that in practice, XGBoost/LightGBM often outperform RF but RF is faster to tune.

Boosting is a sequential ensemble method where each new model corrects the errors of its predecessors. The ensemble builds up additively: F(x) = f₁(x) + f₂(x) + ... + fₘ(x). Model fₘ₊₁ is trained to correct the residual errors of the current ensemble Fₘ. Two implementations: AdaBoost (adaptive boosting) — reweights misclassified examples so the next model focuses on them. Gradient Boosting — fits the next model to the negative gradient of the loss (residuals for MSE loss), i.e., trains a new tree to predict the error of the current ensemble. Boosting reduces bias (by repeatedly targeting errors) but can increase variance (sensitive to noisy data — keeps fitting hard/noisy examples). Learning rate (shrinkage) scales the contribution of each tree: F += η × fₘ. Small η requires more trees but often generalises better. In practice: always use early stopping with a validation set to prevent overfitting.

Boosting = sequential, each model fixes previous errors. Bias reduction. Compare: Bagging = parallel, each model independent. Variance reduction. Boosting is more powerful but more sensitive to noise and hyperparameter choices. The winning recipe: Gradient Boosting + learning rate 0.01-0.1 + early stopping + good data > almost every other approach for tabular data.

qas.q: Why is boosting sensitive to outliers and noisy data?
qas.a: Boosting progressively increases the weight of misclassified examples (AdaBoost) or focuses on residuals (Gradient Boosting). Outliers and noisy examples are consistently hard to classify correctly, so they receive ever-increasing attention. The model ends up overfitting to noise. Mitigation: subsampling (use a fraction of data per tree), small learning rate, max_depth constraints.

qas.q: What is the role of the learning rate in boosting?
qas.a: Learning rate η (also called shrinkage) scales the contribution of each tree: F += η × fₘ. Small η → each tree contributes less → need more trees (higher n_estimators) → slower convergence but better regularisation. Large η → aggressive steps → faster but risks overshooting and overfitting. Typical values: 0.01-0.3. Rule of thumb: lower lr + more trees > higher lr + fewer trees for final performance.

blocks.text: Boosting questions lead to gradient boosting and XGBoost. The conceptual foundation: boosting fits trees to residuals (errors), each tree learns what the previous couldn't. Know that learning rate and n_estimators are inversely related — tuning both together is essential.

Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM) generalise boosting by framing it as optimisation in function space. At each step, we fit a tree to the negative gradient of the loss function. For MSE loss, the negative gradient is the residual (y − ŷ). For log-loss, it's the difference between true labels and predicted probabilities. This allows GBM to work with any differentiable loss. XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) improves vanilla GBM with: second-order gradient approximation (uses Hessian for better optimisation), built-in L1/L2 regularisation (lambda, alpha), column and row subsampling (like Random Forest), tree pruning using a depth-first approach with pruning (unlike greedy GBM), parallel tree construction (split finding is parallelised), native handling of missing values. LightGBM further improves XGBoost with leaf-wise (vs level-wise) tree growth and histogram-based split finding — up to 10× faster on large datasets. CatBoost handles categorical features natively with ordered target encoding.

XGBoost key hyperparameters:
  n_estimators     : number of trees (higher + lower lr = better)
  learning_rate    : shrinkage (0.01-0.3, lower = more regularised)
  max_depth        : tree depth (3-8 for GBM, shallower than RF)
  min_child_weight : minimum hessian sum in a leaf (regularisation)
  subsample        : fraction of rows per tree (0.6-0.9)
  colsample_bytree : fraction of features per tree (0.5-0.9)
  lambda           : L2 regularisation on leaf weights
  alpha            : L1 regularisation on leaf weights
  gamma            : minimum loss reduction for split

XGBoost wins: handles missing values natively, built-in regularisation, faster than vanilla GBM, interpretable feature importance. LightGBM wins for: very large datasets (leaf-wise = faster, less memory). CatBoost wins for: high-cardinality categoricals without preprocessing. Default choice for tabular ML competitions: try XGBoost or LightGBM first.

qas.q: What makes XGBoost better than vanilla Gradient Boosting?
qas.a: XGBoost uses second-order Taylor expansion (uses both gradient and Hessian) for more accurate tree fitting. It adds L1/L2 regularisation directly to the loss function. It supports row and column subsampling (like Random Forest) for more variance reduction. It handles missing values by learning the best direction for them. It parallelises within-tree computation (split finding). Together these make it faster, more regularised, and more accurate.

qas.q: What is leaf-wise vs level-wise tree growth?
qas.a: Level-wise (XGBoost/GBM): grow all leaves at the same depth simultaneously — balanced trees, more robust to overfitting. Leaf-wise (LightGBM): grow the leaf with the highest gain regardless of depth — unbalanced trees, faster and often lower loss, but more prone to overfitting on small datasets. LightGBM's leaf-wise strategy with depth constraints gives a good balance for large datasets.

qas.q: How would you handle a dataset with 10M rows in XGBoost/LightGBM?
qas.a: LightGBM is preferred for large datasets — histogram-based split finding reduces memory from O(n) to O(bins) and is much faster. Use subsampling (subsample=0.7, colsample_bytree=0.7) to further reduce memory and training time. Reduce n_estimators with early stopping. Consider feature engineering to reduce dimensionality. XGBoost with tree_method='hist' also supports large datasets efficiently.

blocks.text: XGBoost/LightGBM knowledge is mandatory for data science roles at tech companies. In any ML project discussion, mention that you would try tree ensembles (XGBoost/LGBM) before neural networks for tabular data — they are faster to iterate on, require less preprocessing, and often match or beat neural networks on structured data.

Stacking (stacked generalisation) trains multiple diverse base models (level-0), then trains a meta-model (level-1) on their predictions. The meta-model learns how to best combine the base model outputs. Implementation: (1) Split training data into K folds. (2) For each fold, train base models on the other K-1 folds and predict on the held-out fold. (3) Stack predictions from all folds — these out-of-fold predictions are the training set for the meta-model. (4) Train meta-model on this stacked matrix. (5) For test: average base model predictions (trained on full training set) and feed to meta-model. Blending is simpler: split training into train/validation; train base models on train, predict validation; train meta-model on validation predictions. Risk: information leakage if base models see the meta-model's validation data. Stacking is powerful but complex — commonly used in competition ML (Kaggle).

Stacking = meta-learning. Base models learn the data; meta-model learns which base models to trust and when. Diversity of base models is crucial: different algorithms (XGBoost, Neural Net, Logistic Regression) outperform using the same algorithm multiple times. Simple meta-model (linear or logistic regression) often better than complex ones.

qas.q: Why use out-of-fold predictions when training a stacking ensemble?
qas.a: If you train base models on the full training set and use their predictions as meta-features, the meta-model sees predictions that are overfit (base models saw those examples during training — predictions are too confident). Out-of-fold (OOF) predictions are made on held-out data the base model never saw during its own training — they represent true generalisation and give the meta-model honest signals to learn from.

blocks.text: Stacking is mostly a competition technique. In production, the complexity rarely justifies the marginal gain. Know the concept and the OOF implementation trick. If asked about stacking in an interview, mention: it's powerful but complex, requires careful CV to avoid leakage, and XGBoost with good tuning often achieves similar results with less engineering.

PCA is an unsupervised dimensionality reduction technique that finds a new set of orthogonal axes (principal components) that maximise the variance of the data. Each principal component (PC) is a linear combination of the original features. PC1 captures the most variance, PC2 the second most (orthogonal to PC1), and so on. Algorithm: (1) Standardise the data (zero mean, unit variance). (2) Compute the covariance matrix. (3) Compute eigenvectors and eigenvalues. (4) Sort eigenvectors by eigenvalue (largest = PC1). (5) Project data onto top k eigenvectors. Explained variance ratio: the proportion of total variance captured by each component. Choose k to retain 95% (or 99%) of variance. Assumptions: linear relationships between features. Does not work for non-linear structure (use t-SNE or autoencoders). Advantages: noise reduction, removes multicollinearity, speeds up downstream models, enables visualisation (2D/3D projection).

Imagine a 3D cloud of data shaped like a flat oval disc tilted in space. PCA finds the orientation of the disc (the direction of maximum spread = PC1), the secondary spread direction (PC2), and the thin direction (PC3 — mostly noise). By projecting to PC1 and PC2, you capture the disc's shape in 2D with minimal information loss — you've compressed 3D to 2D without losing the structure.

PCA workflow in sklearn:
  from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
  from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

  scaler = StandardScaler()
  X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)    # step 1: standardise

  pca = PCA(n_components=0.95)               # retain 95% variance
  X_reduced = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

  print(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)       # variance per component
  print(X_reduced.shape)                     # (n_samples, k_components)

PCA must be fit on training data only, then transform both train and test (same data leakage rule as scalers). PCA loses interpretability — components are combinations of original features. Use PCA for: removing multicollinearity before linear models, noise reduction, visualisation, speeding up ML. Do NOT use PCA for feature selection (use Lasso instead).

qas.q: What is explained variance ratio in PCA and how do you use it?
qas.a: The explained variance ratio of PC_k is the fraction of total variance captured by that component: λₖ / Σλᵢ. Cumulative explained variance: sum ratios until you reach your threshold (e.g., 95%). The corresponding number of components k is your dimensionality reduction target. Plot a scree plot (variance vs component number) — the elbow suggests a natural cutoff.

qas.q: Why must you standardise data before PCA?
qas.a: PCA finds directions of maximum variance. If one feature has variance 1,000,000 (income in dollars) and another has variance 1 (binary flag), PCA will place the first PC almost entirely along the income axis — dominating purely because of scale, not importance. Standardisation (zero mean, unit std) ensures all features contribute equally to the variance maximisation.

qas.q: What is the difference between PCA and LDA?
qas.a: PCA is unsupervised — it maximises overall variance without considering class labels. LDA is supervised — it maximises the ratio of between-class variance to within-class variance. PCA finds the directions of maximum spread in the data. LDA finds the directions that best separate the classes. LDA is specifically for classification preprocessing; PCA is general-purpose. LDA is limited to K-1 components (K = number of classes).

blocks.text: PCA is asked in 80% of ML interviews touching dimensionality. Know: eigenvectors/eigenvalues conceptually, explained variance ratio, always standardise first, PCA ≠ feature selection, PCA not for non-linear data. The insight that separates deep answers: PCA decorrelates features (components are orthogonal) — this directly solves multicollinearity for linear models.

Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) is a supervised dimensionality reduction technique that finds a projection maximising class separability. LDA seeks directions (linear discriminants) that maximise between-class scatter while minimising within-class scatter. Formally: maximise J(w) = wᵀS_B w / wᵀS_W w, where S_B is the between-class scatter matrix and S_W is the within-class scatter matrix. Maximum number of components: K-1 where K = number of classes (binary → 1 component). LDA also serves as a classifier: it assumes Gaussian class distributions with equal covariance matrices (Gaussian discriminant analysis). Compared to PCA: PCA is unsupervised and ignores labels. LDA is supervised and optimises for class separation. LDA often outperforms PCA for classification preprocessing when assumptions hold. Limitation: LDA assumes equal covariance matrices per class — violated for many real datasets.

LDA = supervised PCA for classification. PCA maximises total variance. LDA maximises between-class variance / within-class variance. Use LDA when: you have class labels, classes are Gaussian, covariances are roughly equal. For non-linear class boundaries, kernel LDA or non-linear methods (t-SNE, UMAP) are better.

qas.q: When would you choose LDA over PCA for dimensionality reduction?
qas.a: LDA when: you have class labels and want to optimise for classification, classes are roughly Gaussian, you need exactly K-1 discriminant components. PCA when: you have no labels (unsupervised), you want to preserve overall variance, you need more than K-1 dimensions. In practice, PCA + a classifier often matches LDA-as-preprocessor unless LDA's assumptions hold well.

qas.q: What are the assumptions of LDA?
qas.a: Features are normally distributed within each class. Classes share the same covariance matrix (homoscedasticity of classes). Features are not perfectly correlated. If covariance matrices differ across classes, use Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA) which estimates separate covariances but requires more data and is more prone to overfitting.

blocks.text: LDA is often asked alongside PCA as a comparison. Key differentiator: 'LDA uses class labels, PCA does not.' Know the K-1 components limit and the equal-covariance assumption. In practice, LDA preprocessing is less common than PCA + downstream classifier, but understanding it shows theoretical depth.

t-SNE is a non-linear dimensionality reduction technique designed for visualisation of high-dimensional data in 2D or 3D. It converts high-dimensional Euclidean distances into conditional probabilities representing similarity, then finds a low-dimensional embedding that preserves those probabilities. Algorithm: (1) In high-D, compute pairwise similarities using a Gaussian kernel. (2) In low-D, compute pairwise similarities using a t-distribution (heavier tails than Gaussian — prevents crowding). (3) Minimise KL-divergence between the two similarity distributions using gradient descent. Key property: t-SNE preserves local structure very well — nearby points in high-D stay nearby in 2D. Global structure (inter-cluster distances) is NOT preserved meaningfully. Hyperparameter perplexity (5-50) controls the bandwidth of the Gaussian — roughly the number of effective neighbours. t-SNE is slow (O(n²)) and non-deterministic. NOT suitable for dimensionality reduction before ML — use only for visualisation.

t-SNE is like crumpling a complex 3D structure (galaxy, protein) into a flat 2D drawing while preserving which atoms are close neighbours. Distant atoms may not map meaningfully — you cannot use their 2D distances to infer 3D distances. But local neighbourhoods (what's next to what) are faithfully preserved, revealing clusters and substructures invisible in the full dimensionality.

t-SNE ONLY for visualisation. The 2D plot shows clusters but inter-cluster distances are meaningless. Never use t-SNE as preprocessing for ML — it changes with each run (non-deterministic), does not support out-of-sample projection (no transform() for new data), and distorts global structure. Use UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) as a faster, more scalable alternative that better preserves global structure.

qas.q: What does t-SNE preserve and what does it NOT preserve?
qas.a: Preserves: local structure — nearby points in high dimensions stay nearby in the 2D embedding. Good for identifying clusters and local neighbourhood patterns. Does NOT preserve: global structure — the distances between clusters in the 2D plot are not meaningful. A cluster that appears far from another in 2D may actually be close in the original high-dimensional space.

qas.q: Why is the t-distribution used in the low-dimensional space?
qas.a: The Gaussian distribution has thin tails. When compressing from high-D to 2D, a Gaussian penalty crushes moderately similar points too close together ('crowding problem'). The t-distribution's heavier tails allow moderately similar points to be placed farther apart, preventing all clusters from collapsing to the centre.

blocks.text: t-SNE is asked to check understanding of its limitations. The key answer: visualisation only, local structure preserved, global structure meaningless. The follow-up: 'What is better than t-SNE?' — UMAP (faster, preserves global structure better, supports out-of-sample projection). Mentioning UMAP shows you follow current ML practice.
